title: hospital_surge_management
description: Regional trauma centre faces a 3x surge in patient volume following a multi-site mass casualty incident. Manage bed capacity, coordinate hospital diversion, and open an alternate care site.

timeline.title: Surge Notification
timeline.description: Regional EMS dispatch notifies your trauma centre of a multi-site MCI — a building collapse and a bus crash occurring simultaneously. Estimated 60+ casualties inbound within 90 minutes.

timeline.title: ED at Capacity
timeline.description: Emergency Department reaches 100% capacity. Wait times exceed 4 hours. Three ambulances are holding in the bay unable to offload patients.

timeline.title: ICU Overflow
timeline.description: ICU is full. Two ventilator-dependent patients cannot be transferred to step-down — all beds occupied. Surgeons report two critical MCI patients requiring ICU post-op.

timeline.title: Diversion Request
timeline.description: Hospital B requests your facility go on diversion. Regional coordination centre asks for your current status and decision within 5 minutes.

timeline.title: Alternate Care Site Activation
timeline.description: Community centre adjacent to the hospital is confirmed available as an alternate care site. Staff and supply deployment is pending your authorization.

timeline.title: Mutual Aid Request
timeline.description: Blood bank reports only a 4-hour supply of O-negative remaining. Regional health authority can dispatch an emergency resupply in 2 hours if requested immediately.

timeline.title: Electives Cancelled
timeline.description: OR scheduler requests direction on 8 elective surgeries scheduled for tomorrow. Cancelling frees 3 OR suites and 12 post-op beds.

node-1.title: Initial Surge Response
node-1.situation: You are the Hospital Incident Commander. EMS notifies you of a multi-site MCI generating 60+ casualties. Current ED occupancy is 85%. You have 30 minutes before the first wave arrives.
node-1.question: What is your first action as hospital incident commander?

options.label: A
options.text: Activate the Hospital Emergency Operations Plan, declare an internal disaster, and convene the Incident Management Team immediately.

options.label: B
options.text: Wait for the first MCI patients to arrive before activating the emergency plan to avoid premature disruption.

options.label: C
options.text: Notify only the ED charge nurse and let front-line staff manage the influx without declaring an internal disaster.

node-2.title: Bed Capacity Management
node-2.situation: Your bed coordinator reports 40 available beds system-wide: 20 ED, 8 ICU, 12 general ward. Surge projections indicate you need 60 beds within 2 hours.
node-2.question: How do you create surge capacity?

options.label: A
options.text: Initiate a rapid patient flow review: discharge stable patients, cancel elective admissions, and convert conference rooms to overflow treatment areas.

options.label: B
options.text: Accept MCI patients into existing beds and address overcrowding as it develops.

options.label: C
options.text: Divert all MCI patients to other hospitals and keep your facility at current capacity.

node-3.title: Hospital Diversion Decision
node-3.situation: ED is now at 110% occupancy. Three ambulances are holding in the bay. Regional coordination is asking whether your facility should go on diversion. Hospital B can accept non-critical patients.
node-3.question: What is your diversion decision?

options.label: A
options.text: Go on partial diversion: redirect ambulatory and minor-injury patients to Hospital B while maintaining capacity for critical MCI trauma patients at your trauma centre.

options.label: B
options.text: Refuse all diversion — your hospital must accept all patients regardless of capacity.

options.label: C
options.text: Go on full diversion, redirecting all incoming patients including critical trauma to other facilities.

node-4.title: Alternate Care Site Activation
node-4.situation: The community centre is confirmed available as an alternate care site with capacity for 50 additional patients. Activation requires deploying nursing staff, portable equipment, and supplies.
node-4.question: Do you activate the alternate care site and how?

options.label: A
options.text: Authorize activation immediately: deploy a physician lead, two nurses, portable monitoring, and medications formulary to the alternate site per the pre-established ACS protocol.

options.label: B
options.text: Wait to see if hospital surge resolves before activating the alternate care site.

options.label: C
options.text: Activate the alternate care site but staff it only with volunteers and no physician oversight.

node-5.title: Mutual Aid Request
node-5.situation: Blood bank inventory is critically low — 4 hours of O-negative remaining. An emergency resupply is 2 hours away if requested now. The surgical team has 6 MCI patients requiring blood products.
node-5.question: How do you manage the blood bank shortage?

options.label: A
options.text: Request the emergency resupply immediately, activate massive transfusion protocol, and notify surgeons to conserve O-negative for life-threatening haemorrhage only.

options.label: B
options.text: Continue using blood products at the current rate and reassess in 3 hours.

options.label: C
options.text: Suspend all surgeries until blood bank is restocked to avoid depleting the supply.

node-6.title: Elective Cancellation
node-6.situation: OR scheduler is asking for direction on 8 elective procedures scheduled for tomorrow. Cancellation frees 3 OR suites and 12 post-op beds. Two of the electives are time-sensitive cancer surgeries.
node-6.question: How do you handle elective cancellation?

options.label: A
options.text: Cancel all discretionary electives, defer them to next week, and consult the surgical oncologist about the two cancer cases — only proceed if delay would cause significant patient harm.

options.label: B
options.text: Cancel all elective surgeries including the time-sensitive cancer cases without consultation.

options.label: C
options.text: Proceed with all elective procedures as scheduled and manage MCI patients with the remaining OR capacity.

node-7.title: Supply Shortage Management
node-7.situation: Ventilator supply is at capacity — 12 in use, 0 in reserve. Two MCI patients require mechanical ventilation. Logistics reports 3 ventilators can arrive in 4 hours from a regional cache.
node-7.question: How do you manage the ventilator shortage?

options.label: A
options.text: Request the regional cache immediately, implement ventilator allocation criteria per the Crisis Standards of Care policy, and identify patients who may be weaned early.

options.label: B
options.text: Do nothing and allow ICU to manage the shortage independently without a formal allocation policy.

options.label: C
options.text: Transfer the two MCI patients requiring ventilation to another facility without first checking receiving capacity.

node-8.title: Documentation and Demobilization
node-8.situation: The surge has peaked. Patient flow is stabilizing. Alternate care site has accepted 35 patients. Blood resupply has arrived. Your team needs debriefing and documentation before demobilizing.
node-8.question: What is your final step in the hospital surge response?

options.label: A
options.text: Conduct a hot debrief with incident management team, complete the incident log with timeline and decisions, and initiate a structured after-action review process per HSEEP guidelines.

options.label: B
options.text: Stand down the emergency operations immediately once patient volumes normalize without documentation.

node-9.title: Scenario Complete
node-9.situation: Outstanding leadership. Your hospital successfully managed a 3x patient surge: activated the emergency operations plan, implemented partial diversion, opened an alternate care site, secured blood product resupply, and cancelled elective procedures appropriately. All critical decisions were documented for the after-action review.
